question_types.title: Constant-acceleration 1D kinematics (algebraic)

questions.classification_note: v=v0+at to find time to apex

ordered_content.value: A ball is thrown upward with an initial velocity of 20 m/s. How long will the ball take to reach its maximum height?

questions.classification_note: v²=v0²+2ax for stopping distance from given numbers

ordered_content.value: An airplane lands on a runway with a velocity of 150 m/s. How far will it travel until it stops if its rate of deceleration is constant at −3 m/s2?

questions.classification_note: v=v0+gt for downward throw

ordered_content.value: A ball is thrown downward from the top of a roof with a speed of 25 m/s. After 2 s, its velocity will be
An object has an initial velocity of 15 m/s. How long must it accelerate at a constant rate of 3 m/s2 before its average velocity is equal to twice its initial velocity?

questions.classification_note: Two-stone simultaneous-arrival kinematics

ordered_content.value: A stone is dropped from a 75-m-high building. When this stone has dropped 15 m, a second stone is thrown downward with an initial velocity such that the two stones hit the ground at the same time. What was the initial velocity of the second stone?

question_types.title: Vertical throw: maximum height proportional to v²/(2g)

questions.classification_note: Two-stage: powered ascent + free-fall to apex

ordered_content.value: A rocket is propelled upward with an acceleration of 25 m/s2 for 5 s. After that time, the engine is shut off, and the rocket continues to move upward. The maximum height, in meters, that the rocket will reach is

(A) 900
(B) 1,000
(C) 1,100
(D) 1,200

questions.classification_note: Football pass + receiver pursuit — projectile motion + relative

ordered_content.value: A football quarterback throws a pass to a receiver at an angle of 25 degrees to the horizontal and at an initial velocity of 25 m/s. The receiver is initially at rest 30 m from the quarterback. The instant the ball is thrown, the receiver runs at a constant velocity to catch the pass. In what direction and at what speed should he run?

question_types.title: Relative motion, average speed/velocity, and 1D sign conventions

questions.classification_note: Boat-river crossing — vector sum of velocities

ordered_content.value: The operator of a boat wishes to cross a 5-km-wide river that is flowing to the east at 10 m/s. He wants to reach the exact point on the opposite shore 15 min after starting. With what speed and in what direction should the boat travel?

(A) 11.2 m/s at 26.6° E of N
(B) 8.66 m/s at 63.4° W of N
(C) 11.4 m/s at 60.9° W of N
(D) 8.66 m/s at 26.6° E of N

questions.classification_note: Conceptual: avg vs instantaneous velocity

ordered_content.value: If the average velocity of an object is nonzero, does this mean that the instantaneous velocity of the object can never be zero? Explain.

questions.classification_note: Conceptual: avg velocity zero, avg speed nonzero

ordered_content.value: Explain how it is possible for an object to have zero average velocity and still have nonzero average speed.

questions.classification_note: Raindrops on car windshield — frame change

ordered_content.value: A car is moving in a straight line with velocity v. Raindrops are falling vertically downward with a constant terminal velocity u. At what angle does the driver think the drops are hitting the car’s windshield? Explain.

question_types.title: Translating between x-t, v-t, and a-t graph shapes

ordered_content.value:
A particle has the acceleration versus time graph shown below:

If the particle begins its motion at t = 0, with v = 0 and x = 0, make a graph of velocity versus time.

question_types.title: Slope of x-t graph as instantaneous velocity

questions.classification_note: x(t)=3t²+2 — limit of avg velocity → instant

ordered_content.value:
A particle is moving in one dimension along the x-axis. The position of the particle is given, for any time, by the following position function:
x(t) = 3 t2 + 2
(a)Evaluate the average velocity of the particle starting at t = 2 s for Δt = 1, 0.5, 0.2, 0.1, 0.01, 0.001 s.
(b)Interpret the physical meaning of your results for part (a).

question_types.title: Free-fall from rest: distance scales as t²

questions.classification_note: Stone dropped, last-half time relation

ordered_content.value: A stone is dropped from a height h and falls the last half of its distance in 4 seconds. (a) What was the total time of fall? (b) From what height was the stone dropped?

question_types.title: Free-fall conceptual: acceleration is constant regardless of mass/location

ordered_content.value: Explain why a heavier object near the surface of Earth does not fall faster than a lighter object (Neglect air resistance).

question_types.title: Object leaving a circular path (projectile after release)

questions.classification_note: Mass on string overhead, lands after release

ordered_content.value: A mass attached to a string is twirled overhead in a horizontal circle of radius r every 0.3 s. The path is directly 0.5 m above the ground. When released, the mass lands 2.6 m away. What was the velocity of the mass when it was released, and what was the radius of the circular path?

question_types.title: Tension at top/bottom of vertical circle

ordered_content.value: A 0.25-kg mass is attached to a string and swung in a vertical circle whose radius is 0.75 m. At the bottom of the circle, the mass is observed to have a speed of 10 m/s. What is the magnitude of the tension in the string at that point?

question_types.title: Centripetal acceleration and force calculations (a = v²/r)

questions.classification_note: Hilltop apparent weight — N=m(g-v²/r); could also map to bbb55c91

ordered_content.value: A car and driver have a combined mass of 1,000 kg. The car passes over the top of a hill that has a radius of curvature equal to 10 m. The speed of the car at that instant is 5 m/s. What is the force of the hill on the car as it passes over the top?

(A) 7,300 N up
(B) 7,300 N down
(C) 12,300 N up
(D) 12,300 N down

question_types.title: Horizontal kinetic friction numerical comparison

questions.classification_note: Puck onto frictional surface, stopping distance via μ

ordered_content.value: A hockey puck with a mass of 0.3 kg is sliding along ice that can be considered frictionless. The puck’s velocity is 20 m/s. The puck now crosses over onto a floor that has a coefficient of kinetic friction equal to 0.35. How far will the puck travel across the floor before it stops?

question_types.title: Ideal spring / Hooke's law and effective spring constant

questions.classification_note: Spring on incline — F=kx with weight component

ordered_content.value: A spring with a stiffness constant k = 50 N/m has a natural length of 0.45 m. It is attached to the top of an incline that makes a 30° angle with the horizontal. The incline is 2.4 m long. A mass of 2 kg is attached to the spring, causing it to be stretched down the incline. How far down the incline does the end of the spring rest?

question_types.title: Block on inclined plane with friction (weight components)

questions.classification_note: Find μ_k from observed slide

ordered_content.value: A mass, M, is released from rest on an incline that makes a 42° angle with the horizontal. In 3 s, the mass is observed to have gone a distance of 3 m. What is the coefficient of kinetic friction between the mass and the surface of the incline?

question_types.title: Stacked blocks & friction-limited acceleration

ordered_content.value:
A 3-kg block is placed on top of a 7-kg block as shown below.
[IMG alt= src=../images/f087-04.jpg]
The coefficient of kinetic friction between the 7-kg block and the surface is 0.35. A horizontal force F acts on the 7-kg block.
(a)Draw a free-body diagram for each block.
(b)Calculate the magnitude of the applied force F necessary to maintain an acceleration of 5 m/s2.
(c)Find the minimum coefficient of static friction necessary to prevent the 3-kg block from slipping.

question_types.title: Banked / rotating wall & friction for circular motion

questions.classification_note: Banked curve, no-friction angle

ordered_content.value:
A curved road is banked at an angle θ, such that friction is not necessary for a car to stay on the road. A 2,500-kg car is traveling at a speed of 25 m/s, and the road has a radius of curvature equal to 40 m.
(a)Draw a free-body diagram for the situation described above.
(b)Find angle θ.
(c)Calculate the magnitude of the force that the road exerts on the car.

questions.classification_note: Rotor ride — exactly QT note example

ordered_content.value:
The “rotor” is an amusement park ride that can be modeled as a rotating cylinder, with radius r. A person inside the rotor is held motionless against the sides of the ride as it rotates with a certain velocity. The coefficient of static friction between a person and the sides is µ.
(a)Derive a formula for the period of rotation T, in terms of r, g, and µ.
(b)If r = 5 m and µ = 0.5, calculate the value of the period T in seconds.
(c)Using the answer to part (b), calculate the angular velocity ω in radians per second.

questions.classification_note: Earth-equator apparent weight (rotating frame)

ordered_content.value: How does the rotation of Earth affect the apparent weight of a 1-kg mass at the equator?

questions.classification_note: Free-fall in orbit = weightlessness

ordered_content.value: Explain why objects in orbit appear to be “weightless.”

question_types.title: Newton's third law (force pairs)

questions.classification_note: Why action-reaction pairs allow motion

ordered_content.value: If forces occur in action-reaction pairs that are equal and opposite, how is it possible for any one force to cause an object to move?

question_types.title: Newton's law of universal gravitation (g, F, 1/r^2 scaling)

ordered_content.value: What is the value of g at a height above Earth’s surface that is equal to the radius of Earth?
A planet has half the mass of Earth and half the radius. Compared to the acceleration due to gravity near the surface of Earth, the acceleration of gravity near the surface of this other planet is

(A) twice as much
(B) one-fourth as much
(C) half as much
(D) the same

questions.classification_note: g from density+radius

ordered_content.value: Which of the following is an expression for the acceleration of gravity at the surface of a planet with uniform density ρ and radius r?

(A) G(4πρ/3r2)
(B) G(4πρr2/3)
(C) G(4πρ/3r)
(D) G(4πrρ/3)

questions.classification_note: Orbital velocity at altitude

ordered_content.value: What is the orbital velocity of a satellite at a height of 300 km above the surface of Earth? (The mass of Earth is approximately 6 × 1024 kg, and its radius is 6.4 × 106 m.)

questions.classification_note: Escape velocity — typically energy-based (Unit 3); current Unit-2 QT is a stretch

ordered_content.value: What is the escape velocity from the Moon, given that the mass of the Moon is 7.2 × 1022 kg and its radius is 1.778 × 106 m?

questions.classification_note: Schwarzschild radius — same caveat as q5

ordered_content.value: A black hole theoretically has an escape velocity that is greater than or equal to the velocity of light (3 × 108 m/s). If the effective mass of the black hole is equal to the mass of the Sun (2 × 1030 kg), what is the effective “radius” (called the “Schwarzschild radius”) of the black hole?
What is the gravitational force of attraction between two trucks, each of mass 20,000 kg, separated by a distance of 2 m?
The gravitational force between two masses is 36 N. If the distance between masses is tripled, the force of gravity will be

(A) the same
(B) 18 N
(C) 9 N
(D) 4 N

questions.classification_note: Conceptual — units of g, field-strength language

ordered_content.value: Show that the units for g in N/kg are equivalent to m/s2. Why is g referred to as the “gravitational field strength”?

questions.classification_note: Geosynchronous radius from T=24h

ordered_content.value: Find the orbital radius for a geosynchronous satellite. A geosynchronous satellite is a satellite around Earth that is synchronized, in that its orbital period is 24 hours, just like Earth itself. The mass of Earth is approximately 6.0 × 1024 kg.
